title: Light-powered metabolism in the mammalian eye
summary: Xing et al. report a proof-of-concept study published in Cell that isolates thylakoid grana from plant chloroplasts and repackages them into nanosized vesicles using Pluronic F127 surfactant, creating a system termed LEAF (light-reaction enriched thylakoid NADPH-foundry). When transplanted into mammalian eye cells, these photosynthetic structures generate NADPH independently of mammalian metabolic control without requiring exogenous cofactors. The strategy targets oxidative stress — a key driver in common eye diseases — by leveraging light-driven ATP and NADPH synthesis to counteract cellular damage. This work represents an early-stage convergence of synthetic biology, bioengineering, and ophthalmology that could enable novel light-activated therapeutic approaches for retinal degeneration and other oxidative-damage ocular conditions.
analysis_notes: Content is a Nature Biotechnology preview/summary of a paper published elsewhere (Cell) by Xing et al., written by Iris Marchal. No full peer-reviewed text was accessible for this paywalled article; summary is based on the preview abstract alone, which provides sufficient detail to evaluate significance. Reported as low-priority proof-of-concept work pending preclinical/clinical validation.
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03195-0#content)

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain
the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in
Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles
and JavaScript.

[Access through your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03195-0)

[Buy or subscribe](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03195-0#access-options)

Unlike plant cells, which generate energy through photosynthesis, mammalian cells lack the cellular machinery to convert light into metabolic output. Writing in _Cell_, Xing et al. report transplanting chloroplast structures into mammalian eye cells to help them counteract damage caused by a common eye disease.

The authors isolated thylakoid grana — the stacked disc-like compartments inside chloroplasts that drive the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis — and then repackaged them in the surfactant Pluronic F127, which yielded a nanosized thylakoid system called light-reaction enriched thylakoid NADPH-foundry (LEAF). They focused on the photosynthetic production of NADPH, an essential coenzyme and electron donor that acts as a reducing agent to combat oxidative stress. LEAF could generate NADPH without exogenous cofactors and independent of mammalian metabolic control.

This is a preview of subscription content, [access via your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03195-0)

title: Prime assembly uses a Gibson-like strategy for large DNA insertions
summary: Iris Marchal summarizes work by Liu et al. (published in Nature) on prime assembly (PA), a gene editing system designed to overcome key translational hurdles in large DNA insertion with prime editing tools. PA builds on twin prime editing, which generates ~30-nt flaps on opposing DNA strand halves at the target locus; these flaps serve as anchoring points for integrating double-stranded PCR-synthesized DNA donors whose 5-prime ends are resected to anneal precisely into the flaps—a Gibson assembly-inspired workflow. This approach achieved integration of a 0.8 kilobase donor in human cells at efficiencies up to 40%. The platform directly targets two persistent bottlenecks: junctional scar formation at insertion sites and plasmid-induced inflammatory responses from traditional donor delivery methods.
analysis_notes: Content restricted to preview text behind paywall; full article body inaccessible. Summary and analysis based solely on the accessible abstract/preview section describing Liu et al.'s prime assembly work. The Gibson-like strategy using PCR-synthesized, 5-resected DNA donors annealing to twin prime-editing flaps is novel but unvalidated in vivo from this preview alone.
raw_markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03196-z#content)

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain
the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in
Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles
and JavaScript.

[Access through your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03196-z)

[Buy or subscribe](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-026-03196-z#access-options)

A major goal of gene editing is the ability to efficiently insert or replace large DNA sections to treat genetic diseases that are caused by mutations across various loci. Prime editing tools have recently enabled large insertions into the genome but still face translational challenges such as the production of junctional scars at insertion sites and the need for plasmids as donor templates, which can elicit inflammation. As they write in _Nature_, Liu et al. have tackled these challenges with a system called prime assembly (PA).

PA builds on twin prime editing, which creates ~30-nucleotide flaps on opposing strands of genomic DNA. The authors thought that these flaps might be used to insert a DNA sequence that is provided as a double-stranded donor with complementarity to the flap sequences. DNA donors were synthesized by PCR, and their 5′ ends were resected to allow annealing to the prime editing flaps. This design allowed the integration of a 0.8 kb donor in human cells with up to 40% efficiency.

This is a preview of subscription content, [access via your institution](https://wayf.springernature.com/?redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nature.com%2Farticles%2Fs41587-026-03196-z)

title: An approach to retarget the large serine recombinase Bxb1 enables precise integration of large DNA payloads at desired loci
summary: Frank Buchholz (TU Dresden, NCT Dresden, DKFZ) reports a method to reprogram the large serine recombinase Bxb1 for site-specific genome integration at desired loci. The work enables precise insertion of large DNA payloads, extending the utility of this enzyme class beyond its native recognition specificity toward programmable targets. This advances the toolkit available to gene therapy and synthetic biology teams seeking deterministic genomic incorporation of multi-gene constructs or regulatory elements larger than typically accommodated by recombinase-based systems. As a Nature Biotechnology publication from a leading structural biology / Systems Biology lab, the finding signals potential downstream tooling for ABBVIE-style gene-editing platforms and biomanufacturing workflows requiring stable cassette integration. The paywalled status precludes evaluation of scope (e.g., payload size limits, cellular delivery data) but the headline result warrants follow-up tracking.
analysis_notes: Content is a subscription preview only — no abstract, methods, or results text accessible. The single-sentence article headline describes reprogramming Bxb1 (a large serine site-specific recombinase) to enable precise integration of large DNA payloads at user-defined loci. No commercial entities, drug candidates, trials, or deal terms are present in the visible metadata. Priority set to medium pending full-text assessment.
